content.text: What Is Resource Graph Explorer?

Resource Graph Explorer is the web-based query interface for Azure Resource Graph, accessible from the Azure portal. It provides a KQL editor with syntax highlighting, an inline result table, scope selector, saved query management, and the ability to pin results to dashboards or Azure Monitor Workbooks. It is the fastest way to build and test Resource Graph queries interactively before moving them into automation scripts.

attrs.alt: Mockup of the Resource Graph Explorer UI showing the left sidebar with saved queries, the center KQL query editor and results table, the run/save toolbar, and the right scope and pinning panel

content.text: Access Resource Graph Explorer via Azure portal → search 'resource graph' → Resource Graph Explorer. No additional setup is required — if you can read ARM resources in a subscription, you can query it with Resource Graph Explorer.
Explorer Interface Overview

The Resource Graph Explorer UI is divided into four areas: the left sidebar for saved queries (private to you or shared across the organization), the center KQL editor, the results pane below the editor, and the right panel for scope selection and pinning options.
Area
What It Does
Left sidebar — Saved queries
Browse private queries (yours), shared queries (organization-wide), and built-in sample queries provided by Microsoft
KQL editor
Write and edit KQL queries with syntax highlighting and basic autocomplete for table names and common operators
Run toolbar
Run query, save query, share query link, download results as CSV or JSON, open in Azure CLI
Results pane
Table or JSON view of results; supports column sorting and filtering; shows row count and execution time
Scope selector
Choose Directory (all subscriptions), specific subscriptions, or a management group as the query scope
Pin / share panel
Pin results to an Azure Dashboard tile, pin to an Azure Monitor Workbook, or copy a shareable link to the query
Built-in Sample Queries

Resource Graph Explorer includes a library of sample queries covering common governance, inventory, and compliance scenarios. Access them via Queries > Samples in the left sidebar. The samples cover VM inventory, resources by type, public IPs, NSG rule audits, Kubernetes cluster versions, and policy compliance summaries. Use them as starting points for custom queries.

// Built-in sample queries in Resource Graph Explorer
// (Available under Queries > Samples in the Explorer sidebar)

// Sample 1: Show all resource types and their counts
Resources
| summarize count() by type
| order by count_ desc

// Sample 2: List all Azure regions in use
Resources
| summarize count() by location
| order by count_ desc

// Sample 3: Show resources sorted by name
Resources
| project name, type, location, subscriptionId
| order by name asc

// Sample 4: Get Azure virtual machines sorted by resource group
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| project name, resourceGroup, location,
    vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize),
    osType = tostring(properties.storageProfile.osDisk.osType)
| order by resourceGroup asc, name asc

// Sample 5: Count virtual machines by OS type
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| summarize count() by osType = tostring(properties.storageProfile.osDisk.osType)

// Sample 6: Summarize virtual machines by power state
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| extend powerState = tostring(properties.extended.instanceView.powerState.code)
| summarize count() by powerState
| order by count_ desc

// Sample 7: Kubernetes clusters with their versions
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters'
| extend
    k8sVersion = tostring(properties.kubernetesVersion),
    nodeCount  = toint(properties.agentPoolProfiles[0].count),
    vmSize     = tostring(properties.agentPoolProfiles[0].vmSize)
| project name, resourceGroup, location, k8sVersion, nodeCount, vmSize
| order by k8sVersion asc
Saving and Sharing Queries

Queries can be saved privately (visible only to you) or shared across the organization. Shared queries are ARM resources of type Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries stored in a resource group — anyone with Reader access to that resource group can see and run the query. This makes shared queries a governance artifact: version-controlled (via Terraform or ARM templates), auditable in Activity Log, and accessible to all engineers without requiring each person to rebuild the same query.

# Sharing queries as Azure Resource Graph shared queries
# Shared queries are ARM resources visible to everyone with read access to the RG they're stored in

# Create a shared query via ARM
az resource create \
  --resource-group rg-governance \
  --resource-type Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries \
  --name 'untagged-prod-resources' \
  --properties '{
    "description": "Production resources missing CostCenter tag",
    "query": "Resources | where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources' | where tags['Environment'] =~ 'prod' | where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) | project name, type, resourceGroup, subscriptionId",
    "resultKind": "basic"
  }'

# List all shared queries in a resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-group rg-governance \
  --resource-type Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Description:properties.description}' \
  -o table

# Get a shared query and run it
QUERY=$(az resource show \
  --resource-group rg-governance \
  --resource-type Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries \
  --name 'untagged-prod-resources' \
  --query properties.query -o tsv)

az graph query -q "$QUERY" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Delete a shared query
az resource delete \
  --resource-group rg-governance \
  --resource-type Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries \
  --name 'untagged-prod-resources'
Pinning Results to Dashboards and Workbooks

The pin feature in Resource Graph Explorer creates a live tile on an Azure Dashboard or a Workbook that re-runs the query each time the dashboard loads. A governance dashboard with tiles for untagged resources, non-compliant policy counts, public IP inventory, and unattached disks gives stakeholders real-time visibility into the resource estate without requiring them to run queries manually.

# Integrating Resource Graph Explorer results into dashboards

# Option 1: Pin query result to Azure Dashboard
# In Resource Graph Explorer:
#   1. Write and run your query
#   2. Click 'Pin to dashboard' in the toolbar
#   3. Select or create a dashboard
#   4. Choose tile type: Chart or Grid

# Option 2: Download results as CSV/JSON
# In Resource Graph Explorer:
#   1. Run query
#   2. Click 'Download' → 'Download as CSV' or 'Download as JSON'

# Option 3: Open in Azure CLI (copy the query)
# In Resource Graph Explorer:
#   1. Click 'Open query' → 'Open in Azure CLI'
#   2. Copies az graph query command to clipboard

# Option 4: Query via REST from Power BI
# Power BI Desktop → Get Data → Azure → Azure Resource Graph
# Uses the same KQL query, authenticated via Azure AD

# Automation: schedule query and save to storage via Logic App
# Trigger: Recurrence (daily)
# Action 1: HTTP POST to Resource Graph API (with managed identity auth)
# Action 2: Parse JSON response
# Action 3: Create CSV (from 'select' connector)
# Action 4: Upload blob to storage account

# The REST call in a Logic App HTTP action:
# Method: POST
# URI: https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.ResourceGraph/resources?api-version=2021-03-01
# Authentication: Managed Identity
# Body:
# {
#   "managementGroups": ["mg-corp"],
#   "query": "Resources | where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources' | summarize Count=count() by type | order by Count desc",
#   "options": { "$top": 1000 }
# }
Integrating with Azure Monitor Workbooks

Azure Monitor Workbooks provide a richer dashboard format than Azure Dashboards — they support markdown text, multiple query results on one page, parameter-driven filtering (e.g., filter all tiles by subscription or environment tag), and interactive charts. Resource Graph queries in Workbooks use query type 'Azure Resource Graph' and support all standard visualization types: grid, bar chart, pie chart, time chart, and KPI tiles.

# Pin Resource Graph queries to Azure Monitor Workbooks
# Workbooks let you combine multiple Resource Graph queries with text, metrics, and charts

# Step 1: Create a Workbook (ARM template approach)
cat > /tmp/workbook.json << 'WBEOF'
{
  "$schema": "https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2019-04-01/deploymentTemplate.json#",
  "contentVersion": "1.0.0.0",
  "resources": [
    {
      "type": "microsoft.insights/workbooks",
      "apiVersion": "2022-04-01",
      "name": "[newGuid()]",
      "location": "[resourceGroup().location]",
      "kind": "shared",
      "properties": {
        "displayName": "Governance Dashboard",
        "serializedData": "{\"version\":\"Notebook/1.0\",\"items\":[{\"type\":3,\"content\":{\"version\":\"KqlItem/1.0\",\"query\":\"Resources | where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources' | summarize Count=count() by type | top 10 by Count desc\",\"size\":0,\"queryType\":1,\"resourceType\":\"microsoft.resourcegraph/resources\",\"visualization\":\"table\"}}]}",
        "category": "workbook",
        "sourceId": "/subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTION_ID"
      }
    }
  ]
}
WBEOF

az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --template-file /tmp/workbook.json

# Step 2: Add more tiles via portal (Resource Graph Explorer → Pin to Workbook → select workbook)
# Available visualizations when pinning Resource Graph results:
# - Grid (table)
# - Bar chart (summarize by dimension)
# - Pie chart (count by category)
# - Tiles (single aggregate KPIs)
# - Time chart (requires time column)

# Step 3: Share the workbook
# Portal: Workbooks → Open workbook → Share → Set access to 'Resource Group' or 'Owner'
Keyboard Shortcuts and Productivity Tips
Action
Shortcut / Tip
Run query
Ctrl+Shift+Enter or click Run button
Format query
Shift+Alt+F (auto-indents KQL)
Comment/uncomment selection
Ctrl+/ (adds // prefix to selected lines)
Autocomplete
Ctrl+Space (suggests table names and operators)
Multiple cursors
Alt+Click (useful for editing multiple lines at once)
Download results
Download dropdown → CSV (opens in Excel) or JSON (for automation)
Open in CLI
Run → Open in Azure CLI (copies az graph query command to clipboard)
Pin to dashboard
After running: Pin toolbar button → select dashboard + tile type
Advanced Query Patterns in the Explorer

The Explorer's query editor supports the full KQL surface available in Resource Graph: cross-subscription aggregations, change history via the resourcechanges table, tag compliance sweeps, SKU distribution analysis, and multi-operator pipelines. These patterns go beyond the built-in samples and cover the most common governance and inventory scenarios.

// Advanced query patterns in Resource Graph Explorer

// 1. Cross-subscription resource counts by type and location
Resources
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
| summarize Count = count() by type, location
| order by Count desc
| top 20 by Count

// 2. Resources changed in the last 24 hours (requires Change Analysis add-on)
resourcechanges
| where properties.changeType in ('Create', 'Update', 'Delete')
| extend
    changeType   = tostring(properties.changeType),
    resourceType = tostring(properties.targetResourceType),
    changedBy    = tostring(properties.changedBy),
    changeTime   = todatetime(properties.changeAttributes.timestamp)
| where changeTime > ago(24h)
| project resourceId = id, changeType, resourceType, changedBy, changeTime
| order by changeTime desc

// 3. Find all resources with a specific tag value across all subscriptions
Resources
| where tags['Environment'] =~ 'prod'
| extend
    costCenter  = tostring(tags['CostCenter']),
    owner       = tostring(tags['Owner'])
| project name, type, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, costCenter, owner, location
| order by subscriptionId asc, resourceGroup asc

// 4. VM SKU distribution for right-sizing analysis
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| extend vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize)
| summarize Count = count() by vmSize, location
| extend
    family = extract('^(Standard_[A-Z]+)', 1, vmSize),
    sizeNum = extract('^Standard_[A-Z]+(\\d+)', 1, vmSize)
| order by Count desc

// 5. Resources without any tags (compliance gap)
Resources
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
| where array_length(bag_keys(tags)) == 0 or isnull(tags)
| summarize Count = count() by type, subscriptionId
| order by Count desc
Managing Shared Queries

Shared queries are first-class ARM resources (type: Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries) stored in a resource group. They can be version-controlled as JSON files, bulk-imported via CLI, shared across teams via RBAC on the resource group, and executed programmatically. This makes them the right mechanism for distributing a team's canonical query library.

# Managing shared queries via Azure CLI
# Shared queries are ARM resources stored in a resource group
# They can be pinned to dashboards and shared across teams via RBAC

# Create a shared query (resource group must already exist)
az resource create \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries' \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --name 'prod-vm-inventory' \
  --properties '{
    "description": "All VMs in prod with size and OS type",
    "query": "Resources | where type =~ '"'"'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'"'"' | where tags[Environment] =~ '"'"'prod'"'"' | extend vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize), osType = tostring(properties.storageProfile.osDisk.osType) | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, vmSize, osType, location | order by resourceGroup asc, name asc"
  }'

# List all shared queries in a resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries' \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Description:properties.description}' \
  -o table

# Run a saved shared query by resource ID
QUERY_ID='/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-governance-tools/providers/Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries/prod-vm-inventory'
QUERY_BODY=$(az resource show --ids $QUERY_ID --query 'properties.query' -o tsv)
az graph query -q "$QUERY_BODY" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Grant a team read access to a shared query using RBAC
# (Reader role on the resource group containing the query)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee 'network-team@contoso.com' \
  --role 'Reader' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-governance-tools'

# Export a shared query definition for version control
az resource show \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries' \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --name 'prod-vm-inventory' \
  --query '{name:name, description:properties.description, query:properties.query}' \
  -o json > shared-query-prod-vm-inventory.json

# Bulk import shared queries from a directory of JSON files
for file in ./shared-queries/*.json; do
  NAME=$(python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('$file')); print(d['name'])")
  az resource create \
    --resource-type 'Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries' \
    --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
    --name "$NAME" \
    --properties "$(python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('$file')); print(json.dumps({'description':d['description'],'query':d['query']}))")
  echo "Imported: $NAME"
done
Pinning Results to Dashboards

Any Resource Graph query result can be pinned directly from the Explorer UI or pre-provisioned as a dashboard tile via ARM template. ARM-template dashboards allow governance teams to deploy a standardised operations view (resource counts, compliance gaps, untagged resources) to all subscription owners at once. The tile re-evaluates the query each time the dashboard loads, so the data is always current.
Workbook Integration

Azure Monitor Workbooks accept Resource Graph as a native query data source, allowing you to combine Graph results with Log Analytics metrics and availability data in a single interactive report. A workbook step of type 3 (query) with queryType 1 executes a KQL query against the Resource Graph endpoint, not Log Analytics, even though both are KQL. Export workbooks to JSON for version control and team distribution.

# Integrating Resource Graph with Azure Monitor Workbooks
# Workbooks support 'Azure Resource Graph' as a data source natively.
# Each step section below maps to a workbook 'step' element.

# 1. Create a workbook with a Resource Graph query step via REST API
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
RG='rg-governance-tools'
WB_NAME='governance-workbook'

# Workbook serialized content (simplified — one query step)
# In practice this is a full JSON document authored in the portal and exported
WORKBOOK_CONTENT='{
  "version": "Notebook/1.0",
  "items": [
    {
      "type": 3,
      "content": {
        "version": "KqlItem/1.0",
        "query": "Resources | where type =~ '"'"'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'"'"' | extend vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize) | summarize Count = count() by vmSize | order by Count desc",
        "queryType": 1,
        "resourceType": "microsoft.resourcegraph/resources",
        "title": "VM SKU Distribution"
      }
    }
  ],
  "fallbackResourceIds": ["azure monitor"]
}'

curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/microsoft.insights/workbooks/$WB_NAME?api-version=2022-04-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{
    \"location\": \"eastus\",
    \"kind\": \"shared\",
    \"properties\": {
      \"displayName\": \"Governance Workbook\",
      \"category\": \"workbook\",
      \"serializedData\": $(echo $WORKBOOK_CONTENT | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.dumps(sys.stdin.read()))')
    }
  }" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '\"(name|id)\"'

# 2. Export a workbook to JSON for version control
az monitor app-insights workbook show \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --name $WB_NAME \
  --query 'properties.serializedData' -o tsv > workbook-governance.json

# 3. List all workbooks in a resource group
az monitor app-insights workbook list \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --category workbook \
  --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:properties.displayName}' \
  -o table
Scheduled Reports via Azure Automation

Automation Runbooks can call the Resource Graph Python SDK (azure-mgmt-resourcegraph) using a system-assigned managed identity, eliminating the need to store credentials. Grant the Automation Account's principal the built-in Resource Graph Reader role at the management group scope, then link a runbook to a schedule to produce weekly governance exports, compliance emails, or blob-uploaded JSON reports.

# Scheduling Resource Graph Explorer queries via Azure Automation

# 1. Create an Automation Account with a system-assigned managed identity
az automation account create \
  --name 'aa-governance-reports' \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --location eastus

# Grant the Automation Account's managed identity Reader on the management group
AUTOMATION_PRINCIPAL=$(az automation account show \
  --name aa-governance-reports \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee $AUTOMATION_PRINCIPAL \
  --role 'Reader' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

# Also grant Azure Resource Graph Reader (built-in role)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $AUTOMATION_PRINCIPAL \
  --role 'Resource Graph Reader' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

# 2. Create a Python runbook that runs a Resource Graph query and emails a report
cat > runbook-graph-report.py << 'ENDPY'
import json
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph import ResourceGraphClient
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph.models import QueryRequest, QueryRequestOptions

credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
client = ResourceGraphClient(credential)

query = QueryRequest(
    management_groups=['mg-corp'],
    query="""
        Resources
        | where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
        | where array_length(bag_keys(tags)) == 0 or isnull(tags)
        | summarize UntaggedCount = count() by subscriptionId
        | order by UntaggedCount desc
    """,
    options=QueryRequestOptions(result_format='objectArray')
)
result = client.resources(query)
print(json.dumps(result.data, indent=2))
ENDPY

az automation runbook create \
  --automation-account-name aa-governance-reports \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --name 'Get-UntaggedResources' \
  --type Python3 \
  --content @runbook-graph-report.py

# 3. Create a weekly schedule (Monday 06:00 UTC)
az automation schedule create \
  --automation-account-name aa-governance-reports \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --name 'weekly-monday-0600' \
  --frequency Week \
  --interval 1 \
  --start-time '2026-07-07T06:00:00+00:00'

# 4. Link the runbook to the schedule
az automation job-schedule create \
  --automation-account-name aa-governance-reports \
  --resource-group rg-governance-tools \
  --runbook-name 'Get-UntaggedResources' \
  --schedule-name 'weekly-monday-0600'
Resource Graph Explorer vs CLI vs REST — When to Use Each
Method
Best For
Limitations
Portal Explorer
Ad-hoc investigation, query development, visual result inspection, pinning to dashboards
Manual only; no scripting; max 1000 rows displayed
Azure CLI (az graph query)
Scripts, CI/CD pipelines, local automation, pagination loops, CSV export
Requires CLI installation and az login; not suitable for serverless contexts
REST API (POST /providers/Microsoft.ResourceGraph/resources)
Applications, Logic Apps, Azure Functions, any HTTP client
Must manage auth tokens (Bearer); slightly more verbose than SDK
Python SDK (azure-mgmt-resourcegraph)
Python-based automation, Automation Runbooks, Jupyter notebooks, data pipelines
Additional package dependency; best with managed identity in Azure
PowerShell (Search-AzGraph)
Windows-centric automation, Azure DevOps pipelines, existing PS infrastructure
Requires Az.ResourceGraph module; PS syntax differs from bash
Shared Queries (ARM resource)
Team query libraries, dashboard tiles, cross-team sharing, IaC-deployed canonical queries
ARM API overhead vs ad-hoc; not interactive by itself
Summary

Resource Graph Explorer is the Azure portal's interactive KQL query environment for Resource Graph. Use it to build and test queries before embedding them in automation scripts, save frequently-used queries as shared ARM resources accessible to the whole organization, and pin query results to Azure Dashboards and Monitor Workbooks for continuous governance visibility. The built-in sample queries cover the most common inventory and compliance use cases and serve as useful starting points. The scope selector controls whether queries run against a single subscription, multiple subscriptions, or an entire management group.
